text: An additional trocar is placed for the assistant surgeon to aid in grasping. The J-pouch is gently manipulated and detorsed, revealing correct positioning without twisting. The mesentery shows no intrinsic twisting. Adhesions between the distal J-pouch and pelvis are sharply lysed. The presacral space appears large, potentially contributing to the pouch twisting. The small bowel is examined with gentle grasping, confirming no further obstructions. The transition point between dilated and collapsed bowel is identified, and the J-pouch is returned to its neutral position in the pelvis.

The surgeon performs a rigid sigmoidoscopy to confirm the detorsion of the J-pouch, noting unremarkable mucosa and slight stricturing at the anastomosis, which is gently dilated. The pouch is then desufflated, and a pexy is performed to attach the J-pouch to the pelvic sidewall using seromuscular and superficial bites, ensuring minimal tension. Additional sutures are placed for reinforcement, avoiding vascular structures, and the orientation and laxity of the J-pouch are assessed by lifting the uterus.
